title: Implement viewport state and basic scrolling
description:
**Phase 2: Rendering & Navigation**

Implement viewport state management and basic scrolling commands (up, down, page up, page down, top, bottom).

**Background:**
The viewport is the visible portion of the document. It tracks:
- Current cursor line
- Top visible line
- Viewport dimensions (width x height)

Scrolling commands move the cursor and/or viewport within document bounds.

**Viewport State:**

```rust
pub struct ViewportState {
    pub cursor_line: usize,     // 1-indexed (matches line map)
    pub cursor_col: usize,      // Column position (for horizontal nav)
    pub top_line: usize,        // First visible line
    pub width: u16,             // Terminal width in columns
    pub height: u16,            // Terminal height in rows (minus status/help bars)
}

impl ViewportState {
    pub fn visible_lines(&self) -> Range<usize> {
        self.top_line .. (self.top_line + self.height as usize)
    }
    
    pub fn is_visible(&self, line: usize) -> bool {
        self.visible_lines().contains(&line)
    }
    
    pub fn recenter(&mut self, line: usize) {
        // Put line in middle of viewport
        self.cursor_line = line;
        self.top_line = line.saturating_sub(self.height as usize / 2);
    }
}
```

**Scrolling Commands:**

```rust
pub enum ViewportIntent {
    ScrollUp,           // Move cursor up 1 line
    ScrollDown,         // Move cursor down 1 line
    PageUp,             // Move up one viewport height
    PageDown,           // Move down one viewport height
    HalfPageUp,         // Move up half viewport height
    HalfPageDown,       // Move down half viewport height
    GoTop,              // Jump to line 1
    GoBottom,           // Jump to last line
    ScrollToLine(usize), // Jump to specific line
}
```

**Scrolling Behavior:**

**Simple scroll (j/k):**
- Move cursor one line
- If cursor leaves viewport, scroll viewport to follow
- Cursor stays visible

**Page scroll (PageDown/PageUp):**
- Move viewport by full height
- Move cursor to maintain relative position in viewport
- Or: move cursor by full height (simpler)

**Half-page scroll (Ctrl+d/Ctrl+u):**
- Move viewport by half height
- Cursor follows

**Jump to top/bottom (g/G):**
- Set cursor to first/last line
- Recenter viewport if needed

**Bounds Checking:**
All scroll operations must:
- Clamp cursor to [1, max_line]
- Clamp top_line to valid range
- Never panic on out-of-bounds

Use saturating arithmetic:
```rust
self.cursor_line = self.cursor_line.saturating_sub(1).max(1);
self.cursor_line = self.cursor_line.saturating_add(1).min(max_line);
```

**Cursor Following:**
When cursor moves off-screen, scroll viewport:
```rust
if self.cursor_line < self.top_line {
    self.top_line = self.cursor_line;
}
if self.cursor_line >= self.top_line + self.height as usize {
    self.top_line = self.cursor_line.saturating_sub(self.height as usize - 1);
}
```

**Keymap:**
- j / Down: ScrollDown
- k / Up: ScrollUp
- PageDown / Space / Ctrl+f: PageDown
- PageUp / Ctrl+b: PageUp
- Ctrl+d: HalfPageDown
- Ctrl+u: HalfPageUp
- g: GoTop
- G: GoBottom

**Testing:**
- Scroll up/down in bounds
- Test edge cases: top of doc, bottom of doc
- Page scrolling near boundaries
- Verify cursor stays visible
- Test viewport following cursor
- Property test: scrolling never escapes bounds

**Edge Cases:**
- Document shorter than viewport
- Single line document
- Viewport height changes (terminal resize)
- Very tall viewport (> document height)

**Acceptance:**
- All scroll commands work correctly
- Cursor always stays in bounds
- Cursor always stays visible
- Tests pass for boundary conditions
- Can scroll through fixture documents smoothly

title: Build anchor index with duplicate handling
description:
**Phase 1: Markdown Parsing**

Create anchor index for same-document navigation and implement deterministic duplicate anchor resolution.

**Background:**
Anchors are targets for # links. In markdown, headings automatically create anchors from their slugs. GitHub and other parsers handle duplicate slugs by appending -1, -2, etc. We must match this behavior for compatibility.

**Anchor Sources:**
1. Headings (each heading creates an anchor from its slug)
2. Explicit HTML anchors (optional, future enhancement)

**Duplicate Slug Handling:**
When multiple headings have the same slug:
- First occurrence keeps the base slug
- Second occurrence gets slug-1
- Third occurrence gets slug-2
- Example: three "Overview" headings -> #overview, #overview-1, #overview-2

**Data Structure:**

```rust
pub struct AnchorIndex {
    // Map from slug -> target location
    anchors: HashMap<String, AnchorTarget>,
    // Track slug usage for duplicate numbering
    slug_counts: HashMap<String, usize>,
}

pub struct AnchorTarget {
    pub slug: String,          // Final slug (with -N suffix if needed)
    pub base_slug: String,     // Original slug before duplicate handling
    pub line_number: usize,
    pub heading_id: Option<HeadingId>,
}
```

**Build Algorithm:**
1. Iterate through headings in document order
2. For each heading slug:
   - If first occurrence: add as-is
   - If duplicate: append -{count} and increment count
3. Store final slug in anchor index
4. Update heading's slug field to match final anchor slug

**Why This Matters:**
- User copies #heading link from GitHub
- Opens that markdown file in mdmd
- Expects #heading to jump to the same place
- Without duplicate handling, anchors would be ambiguous

**Edge Cases:**
- Slug that ends in -1 colliding with auto-generated -1
  - "heading-1" and "heading" -> "heading-1", "heading-1-1"
- Empty slugs from empty headings
- Unicode slugs
- Very long slugs (truncation?)

**Testing:**
- Document with duplicate heading text
- Verify first/second/third get correct suffixes
- Test anchor lookup finds correct target
- Test edge case: heading-1 + heading
- Test empty headings
- Property test: anchor lookup is deterministic

**Documentation:**
This is subtle behavior that future maintainers need to understand. Include examples in tests and comments.

**Acceptance:**
- Duplicate headings get -N suffixes
- Anchor lookup finds correct heading
- Behavior matches GitHub's algorithm
- Tests pass for all duplicate scenarios
- Links to #heading-1 work correctly

title: Implement CLI argument parsing with clap
description:
**Phase 0: Bootstrap**

Build command-line interface for mdmd using clap, supporting file paths, stdin, and mode flags.

**Background:**
Users need to invoke mdmd with various options: opening files, reading from stdin, choosing screen modes, and setting initial configuration overrides.

**Requirements:**
- Accept file path argument (positional)
- Support stdin input (detect when no file given)
- Add --inline flag for inline mode (vs default alt-screen)
- Add --link-policy flag (deny/confirm/open)
- Add --help and --version
- Add --config flag for custom config path
- Design for future extension

**Design Considerations:**
- Use clap's derive API for type safety
- Validate file existence early
- Handle - as explicit stdin indicator
- Make defaults align with safe-by-default philosophy
- Clear error messages for invalid options

**Dependencies:**
This task depends on basic project structure being in place.

**Acceptance:**
- mdmd --help shows clear usage
- mdmd file.md opens file
- mdmd < file.md reads stdin
- Invalid flags show helpful errors
- cargo test passes for CLI parsing

title: Implement outline modal UI with hierarchy
description:
**Phase 3: Outline Modal**

Build outline modal widget that displays heading hierarchy with indentation, selection, and filter input.

**Background:**
The outline modal is a centered overlay showing document structure. It displays headings in hierarchical order with visual indentation, highlights the selected heading, and provides a filter input at the top.

**Modal Layout:**
```
┌─ Outline ─────────────────────────────┐
│ Filter: intro_                         │
├────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Introduction (H1)                    │
│ > ├─ Getting Started (H2)              │  ← selected
│   ├─ Installation (H2)                 │
│   │  └─ Prerequisites (H3)             │
│   └─ Configuration (H2)                │
│   Advanced Topics (H1)                 │
│   ├─ Performance (H2)                  │
│   └─ Security (H2)                     │
│                                        │
│ 5 of 124 headings (filtered)          │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Visual Elements:**

1. **Title bar:** "Outline"
2. **Filter input:** Shows current filter query, cursor blinks
3. **Heading list:**
   - Indented by level (2 spaces per level)
   - Tree symbols: ├─ └─ │ for hierarchy
   - Selection indicator: > or highlight background
   - Level indicators: (H1) (H2) etc. or just indentation
4. **Status line:** "N of M headings" or "No matches"

**Indentation Strategy:**
```rust
fn indent_for_heading(heading: &Heading) -> usize {
    (heading.level as usize - 1) * 2
}
```

H1: no indent
H2: 2 spaces
H3: 4 spaces
...

**Tree Symbols (optional, can be simplified):**
- Use ASCII art for tree structure
- Or: simple indentation without lines
- Ensure it works at narrow widths

**Selection Rendering:**
Highlight selected heading with:
- Background color (inverted)
- Arrow indicator (>)
- Both (not color-only for accessibility)

**Filter Input:**
- Show at top: "Filter: [query]_"
- Cursor blinks at end of query
- Shows typed characters in real-time
- Empty query shows all headings

**Scrolling:**
If filtered list exceeds modal height:
- Scroll list to keep selection visible
- Show scrollbar or "... more" indicators
- Selected heading always visible

**Sizing:**
- Width: 60-80% of terminal width (or fixed max like 80 cols)
- Height: 60-80% of terminal height (or fixed max like 30 lines)
- Centered on screen

**Rendering Logic:**
```rust
pub fn render_outline_modal(
    state: &OutlineState,
    doc: &Document,
    viewport: &ViewportState,
) -> Element {
    let modal = Container::new()
        .border(Border::Rounded)
        .title("Outline");
    
    // Filter input
    let filter_input = format!("Filter: {}│", state.filter_query);
    
    // Heading list
    let headings: Vec<_> = state.filtered_headings.iter()
        .enumerate()
        .map(|(idx, &hid)| {
            let heading = doc.find_heading(hid);
            let indent = indent_for_heading(heading);
            let selected = (idx == state.selected_index);
            format_heading_row(heading, indent, selected)
        })
        .collect();
    
    // Status
    let status = format!("{} of {} headings",
        state.filtered_headings.len(),
        doc.headings.len());
    
    modal.child(filter_input)
        .child(headings_list)
        .child(status)
}
```

**Edge Cases:**
- Very long heading titles (truncate with ...)
- Deep nesting (clamp indent at some max)
- Empty filtered results (show "No matches")
- No headings in document (show "No headings")
- Narrow terminal (simplify or hide modal)

**Accessibility:**
- Selection not color-only (use > symbol)
- Keyboard-only navigation (j/k/Enter/Esc)
- Clear focus indicator
- Works without color

**Testing:**
- Render modal with various heading hierarchies
- Test selection highlighting
- Test filter input display
- Test scrolling with many headings
- Snapshot tests at various widths
- Test deep nesting and truncation

**Acceptance:**
- Modal renders correctly
- Hierarchy indentation clear
- Selection visible and accessible
- Filter input works
- Scrolling works with long lists
- Tests pass for layout and rendering

title: Implement outline state model
description:
**Phase 3: Outline Modal**

Build state model for outline modal that tracks filtering, selection, and preview state.

**Background:**
The outline modal (opened with 'o') shows a filterable tree of headings. Users can:
- View document structure at a glance
- Filter headings by text
- Navigate selection with j/k
- Live preview: selecting a heading scrolls the background viewport
- Confirm with Enter, cancel with Esc

**Outline State:**

```rust
pub struct OutlineState {
    pub is_open: bool,
    pub filter_query: String,
    pub filtered_headings: Vec<HeadingId>,  // IDs of headings matching filter
    pub selected_index: usize,               // Index in filtered list
    pub origin_line: usize,                  // Where cursor was when outline opened
    pub origin_top_line: usize,              // Top line when outline opened
    pub preview_line: Option<usize>,         // Line being previewed (if any)
}
```

**Lifecycle:**

1. **Open:** Press 'o'
   - Capture origin (cursor and top_line)
   - Build initial filtered list (all headings)
   - Preselect heading containing cursor
   - Set is_open = true

2. **Filter:** Type text
   - Update filter_query
   - Rebuild filtered_headings (headings matching query)
   - Reset selected_index to 0 (or preserve if still valid)
   - Clear preview

3. **Navigate:** Press j/k
   - Move selected_index within filtered list bounds
   - Update preview_line to selected heading's line
   - Background viewport scrolls to preview (non-destructive)

4. **Confirm:** Press Enter
   - Commit to selected heading
   - Jump cursor to that heading
   - Close modal
   - Clear origin (no longer needed)

5. **Cancel:** Press Esc
   - Restore cursor and viewport to origin
   - Close modal
   - Discard preview changes

**Preselection Logic:**
```rust
fn preselect_heading(headings: &[Heading], cursor_line: usize) -> usize {
    // Find heading that contains cursor (via section lookup)
    // Or: closest heading before cursor
    // Return index in filtered list
}
```

**Filtering Algorithm:**
```rust
fn filter_headings(headings: &[Heading], query: &str) -> Vec<HeadingId> {
    if query.is_empty() {
        return headings.iter().map(|h| h.id).collect();
    }
    
    let query_lower = query.to_lowercase();
    headings.iter()
        .filter(|h| h.title.to_lowercase().contains(&query_lower))
        .map(|h| h.id)
        .collect()
}
```

**Selection Navigation:**
```rust
pub fn move_selection_up(&mut self) {
    self.selected_index = self.selected_index.saturating_sub(1);
    self.update_preview();
}

pub fn move_selection_down(&mut self, max: usize) {
    self.selected_index = (self.selected_index + 1).min(max.saturating_sub(1));
    self.update_preview();
}

fn update_preview(&mut self) {
    if let Some(&heading_id) = self.filtered_headings.get(self.selected_index) {
        // Look up heading line number
        self.preview_line = Some(heading_line);
    }
}
```

**State Invariants:**
- selected_index < filtered_headings.len() (or 0 if empty)
- origin captured when is_open = true
- preview_line references valid line in document
- filter_query and filtered_headings are consistent

**Edge Cases:**
- Filter reduces to zero results
- Empty filtered list (show "no matches")
- Selected heading disappears after filter change
- Document with no headings (modal shows "no headings")

**Testing:**
- Open outline and verify preselection
- Filter headings and verify results
- Navigate selection and verify preview updates
- Confirm jump and verify cursor moved
- Cancel and verify origin restored
- Test filter-to-zero results
- Test document with no headings

**Acceptance:**
- Outline state tracks all necessary fields
- Filtering works correctly
- Selection navigation stays in bounds
- Preview updates on selection change
- Origin capture/restore works
- Tests pass for all state transitions

title: Build link extraction and resolution
description:
**Phase 1: Markdown Parsing**

Extract link references from markdown and implement link resolution logic for same-doc anchors, local files, and external URLs.

**Background:**
Links are first-class navigation in mdmd. Users can Tab through visible links, Enter to follow them. Links can point to:
- Same document anchors (#heading)
- Other markdown files (./other.md or ./other.md#anchor)
- External URLs (https://...)

**Link Model:**

```rust
pub struct LinkRef {
    pub id: LinkId,
    pub label: String,              // Link text
    pub destination_raw: String,    // Raw href from markdown
    pub resolved: ResolvedLink,     // Resolution result
    pub line_number: usize,
    pub start_col: usize,          // Column start (for focus highlighting)
    pub end_col: usize,            // Column end
}

pub enum ResolvedLink {
    SameDocAnchor { slug: String },
    LocalMarkdown { path: PathBuf, anchor: Option<String> },
    ExternalUrl { url: String },
    Unsupported { reason: String },
}
```

**Extraction Algorithm:**
1. Walk events for Event::Start(Tag::Link{..})
2. Capture destination URL and link text
3. Track byte/line/column position
4. Call resolve_link() to determine type

**Resolution Logic:**

```rust
fn resolve_link(destination: &str, current_doc: Option<&Path>) -> ResolvedLink {
    // 1. Check for anchor-only: starts with '#'
    if destination.starts_with('#') {
        return SameDocAnchor { slug: destination[1..].to_string() };
    }
    
    // 2. Parse URL
    match Url::parse(destination) {
        Ok(url) if url.scheme() == "http" || url.scheme() == "https" => {
            ExternalUrl { url: destination.to_string() }
        }
        Ok(url) if url.scheme() == "file" => {
            // Local file URL
        }
        Err(_) => {
            // Relative path
            // Resolve relative to current_doc directory
            // Split on '#' for anchor part
        }
    }
}
```

**Resolution Rules:**
- #anchor -> same doc anchor
- /absolute/path.md -> absolute local file
- ./relative.md -> relative to current doc directory
- ../parent.md -> relative with parent traversal
- https://example.com -> external URL
- file:///path -> local file URL
- Anything else -> Unsupported

**Edge Cases:**
- Links with no destination
- Malformed URLs
- Relative links when reading from stdin (no base path)
- Links containing spaces or special characters
- Anchor-only links in documents without matching anchor
- Circular references (A.md -> B.md -> A.md)

**Security Considerations:**
- Never execute shell commands for URLs
- Validate schemes before opening
- Reject javascript:, data:, and other dangerous schemes
- Canonicalize paths safely (watch for ../ escapes)

**Testing:**
- Extract links from fixture with mixed link types
- Test anchor-only resolution
- Test relative path resolution
- Test external URL detection
- Test unsupported schemes
- Test malformed destinations
- Test stdin mode (no base path)

**Performance:**
- O(n) extraction
- Store resolved links for O(1) lookup during navigation
- No re-resolution unless document reloads

**Acceptance:**
- All link types correctly extracted and resolved
- Tests pass for resolution logic
- Security validation works
- Column positions accurate for focus highlighting

title: Implement display projection cache
description:
**Phase 2: Rendering & Navigation**

Build a display projection cache that converts source markdown to styled terminal output with line wrapping.

**Background:**
The source markdown is the canonical data, but we need to render it styled for display. This involves:
- Syntax styling (headings bold, links underlined, etc.)
- Line wrapping for terminal width
- Caching to avoid re-rendering on every frame

The display projection is a cached view of the source, keyed by viewport width and theme.

**Display Model:**

```rust
pub struct DisplayCache {
    cache: HashMap<DisplayCacheKey, Arc<[DisplayLine]>>,
}

#[derive(Hash, Eq, PartialEq)]
struct DisplayCacheKey {
    doc_id: DocumentId,
    viewport_width: u16,
    theme_id: ThemeId,
    wrap_mode: WrapMode,
}

pub struct DisplayLine {
    pub source_line: usize,        // Which source line this came from
    pub wrap_segment: u16,         // 0 for unwrapped, 0,1,2... for wrapped
    pub plain: Arc<str>,           // Unstyled text
    pub spans: Arc<[StyledSpan]>,  // Style information
}

pub struct StyledSpan {
    pub start: usize,              // Column start
    pub end: usize,                // Column end
    pub style: Style,              // Color, bold, underline, etc.
}
```

**Rendering Pipeline:**
1. Get source line from line map
2. Determine style based on parse metadata:
   - Heading lines: bold, larger or prefixed
   - Link spans: underlined, distinct color
   - Code blocks: monospace, muted color
   - Emphasis: italic/bold
3. Apply line wrapping based on viewport width
4. Create DisplayLine records
5. Cache result

**Wrapping Strategies:**
- No wrap: display lines are 1:1 with source lines (may clip)
- Word wrap: break at word boundaries, honor indentation
- Hard wrap: break at any character (for long URLs, code)

Initial implementation: hard wrap for simplicity, word wrap as enhancement.

**Cache Invalidation:**
Cache key includes:
- doc_id: different per document
- viewport_width: invalidate on terminal resize
- theme_id: invalidate on theme change
- wrap_mode: invalidate on wrap mode toggle

When any of these change, re-render affected lines.

**Incremental Rendering:**
For v2, full re-render on invalidation is acceptable (<30ms for 10MB doc).
Future: track dirty line ranges for incremental updates.

**Style Mapping:**
Based on markdown structure:
- H1: bold + bright color
- H2-H6: bold
- Links: underline + link color
- Code inline: monospace + muted
- Code blocks: monospace + muted background
- Emphasis: italic
- Strong: bold

**Source Mapping:**
Critical invariant: display lines always reference source lines.
- search highlights: find in source, map to display
- navigation: work in source space, render to display
- link focus: source line/col, render on display line

**Testing:**
- Render simple markdown
- Verify styling is applied
- Test line wrapping at various widths
- Test cache key changes trigger re-render
- Test source line mapping is preserved
- Performance: render 1MB doc in <50ms

**Edge Cases:**
- Very long lines (URL with 1000+ chars)
- Empty lines
- Lines with only whitespace
- Unicode width calculations (emoji, CJK)
- Tab characters

**Performance:**
- Cache hit: O(1)
- Cache miss: O(n) with viewport height
- Full document render: O(n) with document size (acceptable on resize)

**Acceptance:**
- Markdown renders with correct styling
- Line wrapping works
- Cache invalidation works correctly
- Source line mapping preserved
- Tests pass for styling and wrapping

title: Release v2.0 preparation and artifacts
description:
**Phase 8: Testing & Release**

Final release preparation: changelog, versioning, packaging, and artifacts.

**Pre-Release Checklist:**
- [ ] All tests passing
- [ ] All benchmarks meet budgets
- [ ] Documentation complete
- [ ] CI green on main branch
- [ ] No known critical bugs
- [ ] Manual QA on all fixtures passed
- [ ] Accessibility audit passed

**Versioning:**
- Set version in Cargo.toml: 2.0.0
- Tag commit: v2.0.0
- Follow semver

**CHANGELOG.md:**
- Format: Keep a Changelog style
- Sections:
  - Added (new features)
  - Changed (changes to existing)
  - Fixed (bug fixes)
  - Security (security fixes)
- Highlight major features:
  - Heading navigation
  - Outline modal
  - Search
  - Link following
  - Session persistence

**Release Artifacts:**
- Binary for Linux x86_64
- Binary for macOS x86_64/arm64
- Source tarball
- Upload to GitHub Releases

**Release Notes:**
- Write user-friendly release notes
- Include screenshots/demos
- Link to documentation
- Installation instructions
- Upgrade notes (if applicable)

**Post-Release:**
- Announce on relevant forums
- Update project homepage
- Close milestone

**Rollback Plan:**
- Keep v1.x branch maintained
- Document rollback procedure
- Keep old binaries available

**Acceptance:**
- v2.0.0 tagged and released
- Artifacts published
- CHANGELOG complete
- Release notes published

title: Implement status bar rendering
description:
**Phase 2: Rendering & Navigation**

Build status bar widget that shows file context, current heading, and position.

**Background:**
The status bar provides orientation in the document. It's always visible at the top of the screen and shows:
- File name or source type
- Current heading context
- Line position (current / total)
- Mode (Normal, Search, Outline, etc.)
- Transient status messages (search results, errors, etc.)

**Status Bar Layout:**
```
[file.md] Section: Introduction (H2) | Line 45/1234 | Normal | "2 matches found"
```

Components (left to right):
1. **Source indicator**: [file.md] or [stdin] or [fixture]
2. **Heading context**: Current section heading (if any)
3. **Position**: Line N / Total
4. **Mode**: Normal, Search, Outline, Help, etc.
5. **Status message**: Transient info (fades after 5s)

**Heading Context Logic:**
```rust
pub fn current_heading_context(doc: &Document, cursor_line: usize) -> Option<&Heading> {
    // Find section containing cursor_line
    let section = doc.sections.iter()
        .find(|s| s.start_line <= cursor_line && cursor_line < s.end_line)?;
    
    // Look up heading for this section
    section.heading.and_then(|hid| doc.headings.iter().find(|h| h.id == hid))
}
```

**Status Message System:**
```rust
pub struct StatusMessage {
    pub text: String,
    pub severity: Severity,
    pub timestamp: Instant,
}

pub enum Severity {
    Info,    // Blue or neutral
    Warn,    // Yellow
    Error,   // Red
}
```

Messages expire after 5 seconds (or until next message).

**Rendering:**
Use frankentui's layout system:
- Reserve first line of screen for status bar
- Use contrasting background (inverted colors)
- Truncate long heading names with "..." if needed
- Right-align position info
- Center or right-align status messages

**Style:**
- Background: inverted (dark bg on light term, light bg on dark term)
- File name: bold
- Heading context: italic or distinct color
- Status messages: color-coded by severity

**Width Handling:**
Status bar must work at narrow widths (80 cols):
- Priority: file name, position, mode
- Truncate heading context if needed
- Hide status message if too narrow
- Use ellipsis for truncation

**Examples:**

80 columns:
```
[README.md] H2: Installation | 45/200 | Normal
```

120 columns:
```
[README.md] Section: Installation (H2) | Line 45/200 | Normal | "Jumped to next heading"
```

Narrow (60 cols):
```
[README.md] H2: Install... | 45/200 | Normal
```

**Dynamic Updates:**
Status bar updates on:
- Cursor movement (position changes)
- Mode changes
- Status message set
- File changes (reload)

**Testing:**
- Render status bar at various widths
- Verify heading context updates with cursor
- Test truncation at narrow widths
- Test status message expiry
- Snapshot tests for layout

**Accessibility:**
- Use symbols + text (not color alone) for severity
- Ensure sufficient contrast
- Status changes are apparent

**Acceptance:**
- Status bar renders correctly
- Shows current heading context
- Position updates on cursor movement
- Truncation works at narrow widths
- Status messages expire correctly
- Tests pass for layout and updates

title: Build section tracking for structural navigation
description:
**Phase 1: Markdown Parsing**

Build section model to represent document structure between headings, enabling section-aware navigation and viewport context.

**Background:**
Sections represent the content between headings. They're useful for:
- Understanding current reading context
- Enabling "jump to section" semantics
- Calculating what's visible in viewport
- Supporting future features (fold sections, section metadata)

**Section Model:**

```rust
pub struct Section {
    pub id: SectionId,
    pub heading: Option<HeadingId>,  // None for pre-first-heading content
    pub start_line: usize,
    pub end_line: usize,              // Exclusive
    pub level: u8,                    // Heading level (0 for preamble)
}
```

**Build Algorithm:**
1. Sort headings by line number
2. Create sections between consecutive headings
3. First section (preamble) runs from line 0 to first heading
4. Each subsequent section runs from heading to next heading or EOF
5. Section inherits level from its heading

**Use Cases:**
- "What section am I in?" -> binary search by line number
- "Show heading context" -> find section, look up heading
- Outline preview -> calculate section bounds
- Future: section folding, section-level navigation

**Edge Cases:**
- Document with no headings (one section covering whole doc)
- Empty sections (consecutive headings)
- Last section (extends to end of document)
- Very short sections (single line)

**Data Structure Considerations:**
- Sections stored as Vec<Section> sorted by start_line
- Binary search for O(log n) lookup by line number
- Reference headings by HeadingId (not by index, handles insertion/deletion)

**Testing:**
- Build sections from fixture with multiple headings
- Test section lookup by line number
- Test document with no headings (preamble only)
- Test consecutive headings (empty sections)
- Test last section extends to EOF

**Performance:**
- O(n) build time with number of headings
- O(log n) lookup time
- Minimal memory overhead (few sections relative to lines)

**Future Enhancements:**
This model can be extended for:
- Section metadata extraction (first paragraph as summary)
- Section folding (hide section content)
- Section-level search ("search only in current section")

**Acceptance:**
- Sections correctly span heading boundaries
- Section lookup by line number works
- Tests pass for edge cases
- Preamble section (before first heading) handled correctly

title: Implement Document model and state management
Setup project structure and Cargo.toml
description:
**Phase 0: Bootstrap**

Initialize the mdmd project with proper Cargo.toml configuration and directory structure.

**Context:**
This is the foundational task for mdmd v2 - a terminal markdown pager built with frankentui. The project follows an Elm-style architecture with strict layering between domain, application, and UI concerns.

**Deliverables:**
- Cargo.toml with frankentui dependency
- Create src/ directory structure:
  - src/main.rs (entry point)
  - src/app.rs (top-level app module)
  - src/cli.rs, config.rs, constants.rs, error.rs
  - src/model/ (app state modules)
  - src/domain/ (pure logic)
  - src/parser/ (markdown parsing)
  - src/render/ (display projection)
  - src/nav/ (navigation logic)
  - src/input/ (keymaps and intents)
  - src/ui/ (widgets)
  - src/infra/ (fs, config loader, etc.)
- Basic README.md and .gitignore

**Architecture Notes:**
The directory structure enforces separation:
- domain: pure functions, no IO
- application: reducers, command scheduling
- ui: rendering only, no business logic

**Acceptance:**
- cargo check passes
- Directory structure matches plan section 3

title: Implement help modal with filterable shortcuts
description:
**Phase 6: Help & Bookmarks**

Build help modal (?) showing all keybindings with filter capability.

**Help Data:**
```rust
struct ShortcutInfo {
    key: String,
    context: String,  // "Normal", "Outline", "Search", "Global"
    action: String,
    description: String,
}
```

**Modal UI:**
- Title: "Help - Keyboard Shortcuts"
- Filter input at top
- Scrollable list of shortcuts
- Columns: Key | Context | Action | Description
- j/k to scroll, Esc/? to close

**Filter:**
- Match on key, context, action, or description
- Case-insensitive substring match
- Live update as user types

**Shortcuts to Document:**
- All core commands (j/k/n/p/o/etc.)
- Mode-specific commands
- Less common commands (R reload, etc.)
- Config overrides reflected if loaded

**Testing:**
- Open help modal
- Filter shortcuts
- Verify all shortcuts listed
- Test at narrow widths

**Acceptance:**
- Help modal shows all shortcuts
- Filter works correctly
- Layout clear and readable
- User can discover all features

title: Implement navigation history (back/forward)
description:
**Phase 5: Link Navigation**

Implement back (b/Ctrl+o) and forward (Ctrl+i) navigation through document history.

**History Model:**
```rust
struct Model {
    doc_stack: Vec<DocumentSession>,
    current_doc_index: usize,  // Index in doc_stack
}
```

**Navigation:**
- Follow link: push new session, current_doc_index = len - 1
- Back: decrement current_doc_index, restore that session
- Forward: increment current_doc_index, restore that session

**Back (b or Ctrl+o):**
- If current_doc_index > 0: move back
- Restore cursor and viewport from previous session
- Status: "Back to: [filename]"
- Else: "No previous document"

**Forward (Ctrl+i):**
- If current_doc_index < len - 1: move forward
- Restore cursor and viewport from next session
- Status: "Forward to: [filename]"
- Else: "No next document"

**Session Restoration:**
```rust
fn restore_session(model: &mut Model, index: usize) {
    let session = &model.doc_stack[index];
    model.current_doc_index = index;
    model.viewport.cursor_line = session.cursor_line;
    model.viewport.top_line = session.top_line;
    // Document is already loaded (Arc clone)
}
```

**Edge Cases:**
- Stack depth limit (max 100 sessions?)
- Memory usage with many large docs
- Same file opened twice (separate sessions)

**Testing:**
- Follow link, press back, verify return
- Multiple forwards, multiple backs
- Test stack boundaries (no back at start)
- Test forward after back

**Acceptance:**
- Back restores previous document and position
- Forward works after back
- Boundaries handled correctly
- Status messages are clear

title: Implement external URL policy enforcement
description:
**Phase 5: Link Navigation**

Implement external link policy: deny (default), confirm, or open with platform opener.

**Policy Types:**
```rust
pub enum LinkPolicy {
    Deny,     // Show message only (default)
    Confirm,  // Prompt user before opening
    Open,     // Open directly (risky)
}
```

**Behavior:**

**Deny (default):**
- Status: "External link: https://... (denied by policy)"
- Link not opened
- Safe by default

**Confirm:**
- Show modal: "Open external link? https://..."
- Options: [Open] [Cancel] (keyboard: o/Esc)
- If confirmed: open via platform opener
- If cancelled: do nothing

**Open:**
- Open directly via platform opener
- Status: "Opened: https://..."
- No confirmation

**Platform Opener:**
```rust
fn open_url(url: &str) -> Result<(), Error> {
    // Use 'open' crate or similar
    // Never use shell: Command::new("xdg-open") is a security risk
    // Validate URL scheme first
}
```

**URL Validation:**
- Only allow http:// and https://
- Reject javascript:, data:, file:, etc.
- Show clear error for unsupported schemes

**Configuration:**
- CLI flag: --link-policy=deny|confirm|open
- Config file: link_policy = "confirm"
- Env var: MDMD_LINK_POLICY

**Testing:**
- Test each policy mode
- Verify default is deny
- Test URL validation
- Test confirmation modal (if confirm mode)
- Test platform opener (manual)

**Acceptance:**
- Policy enforcement works correctly
- Default is deny (safe)
- Confirmation modal works (if confirm)
- Only http/https allowed
- No shell execution (security)

title: Implement Command/Msg pattern foundation
description:
**Phase 0: Bootstrap**

Establish the Elm-style command and message pattern that will drive all async operations in mdmd.

**Background:**
mdmd uses frankentui's Elm architecture. Pure update functions return (Model, Cmd) tuples. Commands represent effects to execute, which eventually produce Messages that feed back into the update loop. This pattern ensures:
- Deterministic state updates
- Testable business logic
- Clear async operation tracking
- Race-free cancellation

**Core Types to Implement:**

```rust
/// Unique identifier for tracking async operations
pub struct RequestId(pub u64);

/// Generation counter for invalidating stale results
pub struct Generation(pub u64);

/// Commands represent effects to execute
pub enum Cmd {
    None,
    Batch(Vec<Cmd>),
    LoadFile { request_id: RequestId, path: PathBuf },
    ParseMarkdown { request_id: RequestId, source: Arc<str> },
    Search { request_id: RequestId, generation: Generation, query: SearchQuery },
    OpenExternalUrl { url: String },
    SaveSession,
    // ... more as features are added
}

/// Messages represent results of commands
pub enum Msg {
    FileLoaded { request_id: RequestId, result: Result<String, Error> },
    MarkdownParsed { request_id: RequestId, result: ParseResult },
    SearchComplete { request_id: RequestId, generation: Generation, matches: Vec<Match> },
    // ... more as features are added
}
```

**Implementation Details:**
- RequestId generator (atomic counter)
- Generation tracker per operation type
- Command executor that runs Cmd and produces Msg
- Pending operations tracking (for cancellation)
- Helper functions for common patterns

**Key Patterns:**
- Always generate RequestId for trackable operations
- Use Generation for cancellable/supersedable operations (search)
- Batch multiple commands when needed
- Commands are data, executor runs them

**Testing Strategy:**
- Test request ID uniqueness
- Test generation increments
- Test command batching
- Test pending operation tracking

**Why This Matters:**
This pattern prevents entire classes of bugs:
- No race conditions from overlapping async ops
- Stale results can't corrupt UI state
- All effects are explicit and trackable
- Business logic stays pure and testable

**Acceptance:**
- Cmd and Msg types compile
- RequestId generator works
- Generation tracking compiles
- Basic executor skeleton exists
- Tests pass

title: Build line map for byte-to-line conversion
description:
**Phase 1: Markdown Parsing**

Create line map data structure for efficient conversion between byte offsets and line/column positions.

**Background:**
Markdown parser (pulldown-cmark) gives us byte offsets. UI and navigation work with line/column positions. We need bidirectional conversion:
- Byte offset -> (line, column) for rendering search highlights
- Line number -> byte range for extracting line text
- Column offset -> byte offset for link focus positioning

**Line Map Model:**

```rust
pub struct LineRecord {
    pub line_number: usize,    // 1-indexed (0 is invalid)
    pub start_byte: usize,     // Byte offset of line start
    pub end_byte: usize,       // Byte offset of line end (exclusive)
    pub text: Arc<str>,        // Cached line text
}

pub struct LineMap {
    lines: Arc<[LineRecord]>,
}

impl LineMap {
    pub fn byte_to_line(&self, byte_offset: usize) -> Option<usize>;
    pub fn line_to_byte_range(&self, line: usize) -> Option<(usize, usize)>;
    pub fn byte_to_line_col(&self, byte_offset: usize) -> Option<(usize, usize)>;
    pub fn line_col_to_byte(&self, line: usize, col: usize) -> Option<usize>;
}
```

**Build Algorithm:**
1. Iterate through source string
2. Track byte offset
3. Split on \n, recording start/end bytes for each line
4. Store line text (Arc for cheap cloning)
5. Handle edge cases: empty lines, no final newline, CRLF vs LF

**Unicode Considerations:**
- Column offsets must be in **characters**, not bytes
- Byte 10 might be character 8 if previous chars are multi-byte
- Use str::chars() for character iteration
- Cache column->byte mapping if performance matters

**Lookup Algorithms:**
- byte_to_line: binary search on start_byte (O(log n))
- line_to_byte_range: direct index lookup (O(1))
- byte_to_line_col: find line, then count chars (O(k) where k=line length)

**Why Arc<str>?**
- Line text is immutable
- Shared across display cache, search results, etc.
- Cheap cloning
- Cache-friendly

**Edge Cases:**
- Empty document (no lines? or one empty line?)
- Document without trailing newline
- Very long lines (10,000+ chars)
- Lines with only whitespace
- Mixed line endings (CRLF vs LF)

**Testing:**
- Build line map from simple document
- Test byte->line conversion
- Test line->byte_range conversion
- Test byte->line_col with unicode
- Test line_col->byte with unicode
- Property test: round-trip conversions

**Performance:**
- O(n) build time with document size
- O(log n) byte->line lookup
- O(1) line->byte lookup
- Memory: O(n) with document size (each line stored once)

**Acceptance:**
- All conversion functions work correctly
- Unicode handling is correct (characters, not bytes)
- Tests pass for edge cases
- Round-trip conversions are lossless
- Performance is acceptable for 10MB documents

title: Implement help bar rendering
description:
**Phase 2: Rendering & Navigation**

Build help bar widget that shows quick reference for key shortcuts at bottom of screen.

**Background:**
The help bar provides immediate discoverability of core shortcuts without opening the full help modal. It's always visible at the bottom, showing context-sensitive shortcuts.

**Help Bar Layout:**
```
n:next p:prev o:outline /:search m:mark ?:help q:quit
```

**Mode-Specific Shortcuts:**

**Normal mode:**
```
j/k:scroll  n/p:heading  o:outline  /:search  Tab:links  ?:help  q:quit
```

**Outline mode:**
```
j/k:move  Enter:jump  Esc:cancel  ?:help
```

**Search mode:**
```
Enter:search  Esc:cancel  Alt+r:regex  Alt+c:case  ?:help
```

**Link focus mode:**
```
Tab:next  Enter:follow  Esc:clear  b:back  ?:help
```

**Rendering Strategy:**
```rust
pub fn render_help_bar(mode: &AppMode, width: u16) -> Element {
    let shortcuts = match mode {
        AppMode::Normal => normal_shortcuts(),
        AppMode::Outline => outline_shortcuts(),
        AppMode::SearchInput => search_shortcuts(),
        // ... etc
    };
    
    // Format as compact list
    // Truncate if too many for width
    format_shortcut_list(shortcuts, width)
}
```

**Width Handling:**
At narrow widths, prioritize core shortcuts:
- Always show: ?:help q:quit
- Show mode-specific actions
- Hide less common shortcuts if needed
- Use shorter labels: "nxt" instead of "next"

80 columns (fits most shortcuts):
```
j/k:scroll n/p:heading o:outline /:search ?:help q:quit
```

60 columns (truncated):
```
j/k n/p o:outline /:search ?:help q:quit
```

**Style:**
- Background: distinct from main content (subtle color)
- Separators: use spaces or pipes (|)
- Key indicators: bold or distinct color
- Low visual weight (not distracting)

**Design Principles:**
- Help bar is for discovery, not reference
- Show most common shortcuts only
- Full help available via ? key
- Don't overwhelm with too many keys
- Make it scannable at a glance

**Testing:**
- Render help bar for each mode
- Verify correct shortcuts shown per mode
- Test truncation at narrow widths
- Snapshot tests for each mode
- Verify mode transitions update help bar

**Accessibility:**
- Text is readable at small sizes
- Sufficient contrast
- Works without color (text labels)

**Future Enhancement:**
- Animate mode transitions
- Highlight recently pressed keys
- Show key hints during mode-specific operations

**Acceptance:**
- Help bar renders for all modes
- Shows mode-appropriate shortcuts
- Truncation works at narrow widths
- Updates on mode change
- Tests pass for all modes

title: Implement heading navigation (n/p)
description:
**Phase 2: Rendering & Navigation**

Implement next-heading (n) and previous-heading (p) navigation with binary search lookup.

**Background:**
Heading navigation is a core feature of mdmd. Unlike line-by-line scrolling, users jump between semantic boundaries (headings). This makes navigating large documents much faster.

**Navigation Logic:**

```rust
pub fn next_heading(doc: &Document, current_line: usize) -> Option<usize> {
    // Find first heading with line_number > current_line
    doc.headings
        .iter()
        .find(|h| h.line_number > current_line)
        .map(|h| h.line_number)
}

pub fn prev_heading(doc: &Document, current_line: usize) -> Option<usize> {
    // Find last heading with line_number < current_line
    doc.headings
        .iter()
        .rev()
        .find(|h| h.line_number < current_line)
        .map(|h| h.line_number)
}
```

**Optimization:**
Since headings are sorted by line_number, use binary search:

```rust
pub fn next_heading_fast(doc: &Document, current_line: usize) -> Option<usize> {
    let idx = doc.headings.binary_search_by_key(&current_line, |h| h.line_number);
    match idx {
        Ok(exact) => doc.headings.get(exact + 1).map(|h| h.line_number),
        Err(insert_idx) => doc.headings.get(insert_idx).map(|h| h.line_number),
    }
}
```

This is O(log n) instead of O(n).

**Boundary Behavior:**
- At last heading, press 'n': show "No next heading" status
- At first heading, press 'p': show "No previous heading" status
- Before first heading, press 'p': show "No previous heading"
- After last heading, press 'n': show "No next heading"
- Keep cursor position unchanged on boundary

**Viewport Update:**
When jumping to heading:
- Set cursor to heading line
- Recenter viewport to put heading in middle of screen
- Update status bar to show heading title

**Status Messages:**
- "Jumped to: [heading title]" on success
- "No next heading" on boundary
- "No previous heading" on boundary
- "Document has no headings" if empty

**Edge Cases:**
- Document with no headings
- Cursor exactly on heading (n goes to next, p goes to previous)
- Consecutive headings (multiple headings on adjacent lines)
- First heading on line 1
- Last heading on last line

**Keymap:**
- n: next heading
- p: previous heading (or shift+n, but p is vi-style)

**User Experience:**
Heading navigation should feel instant:
- No lag (binary search is fast)
- Smooth viewport recentering
- Clear status feedback

**Testing:**
- Navigate forward through all headings
- Navigate backward through all headings
- Test boundaries (no wrap)
- Test document with no headings
- Test cursor on heading line
- Property test: repeated n/p never escapes bounds
- Performance: 10k headings, navigation still instant

**Future Enhancement:**
- Wrap navigation (n at last heading goes to first)
- Level-aware navigation (next H2, skip H3)
- Jump to heading by level (next H1)

**Acceptance:**
- n/p commands work correctly
- Binary search is implemented
- Boundary conditions handled gracefully
- Status messages are clear
- Tests pass for all edge cases
- Can navigate fixture with nested headings

title: Implement session persistence and restore
description:
**Phase 6: Help & Bookmarks**

Implement session state persistence: save recent docs and positions, restore on startup.

**Session Data:**
```rust
struct SessionState {
    recent_files: Vec<RecentFile>,
    bookmarks: HashMap<char, Bookmark>,
    version: u32,  // Schema version
}

struct RecentFile {
    path: PathBuf,
    last_cursor_line: usize,
    last_top_line: usize,
    last_opened: SystemTime,
}
```

**Session File:**
- Location: /mdmd/session.json (or ~/.local/state/mdmd/)
- Format: JSON (serde)
- Schema version for migration

**Save:**
- Save on quit (normal exit)
- Save periodically during use (optional)
- Include current doc position
- Include bookmarks
- Include recent files (last 20)

**Restore:**
- Load on startup
- If opening same file: restore cursor/viewport
- Config flag: auto_restore = true/false
- Handle missing/moved files gracefully
- Version mismatch: use defaults, don't crash

**Error Handling:**
- Missing session file: not an error, use defaults
- Corrupted JSON: log warning, use defaults
- Permission denied: warn, continue without persistence

**Testing:**
- Save session and restore
- Test cursor/viewport restore
- Test with missing file
- Test with corrupted session file

**Acceptance:**
- Session saves on quit
- Session restores correctly
- Missing/corrupt files handled gracefully
- Bookmarks persist across sessions

title: Implement config loader with precedence system
description:
**Phase 0: Bootstrap**

Build configuration system that loads from multiple sources with clear precedence: CLI flags > env vars > config file > defaults.

**Background:**
mdmd needs flexible configuration to support different user preferences while maintaining safe defaults. The precedence system ensures command-line overrides work intuitively.

**Configuration Sources (in precedence order):**
1. CLI flags (highest priority)
2. Environment variables (MDMD_*)
3. Config file ($XDG_CONFIG_HOME/mdmd/config.toml)
4. Hard-coded defaults (lowest priority)

**Configuration Schema:**
- screen_mode: inline | altscreen
- link_policy: deny | confirm | open
- search_case_mode: insensitive | sensitive | smart
- search_regex_enabled: bool
- theme: default | high_contrast | custom
- keymap_overrides: HashMap<String, String>
- session_restore_auto: bool

**Implementation Notes:**
- Use serde for TOML deserialization
- Create typed Config struct with builder pattern
- Handle missing config file gracefully (not an error)
- Validate config values and provide clear errors
- Log effective configuration in debug mode

**Testing Strategy:**
- Unit tests for precedence rules
- Test missing config file handling
- Test invalid config values
- Test env var parsing
- Verify CLI overrides work

**Acceptance:**
- Config loads from all sources correctly
- Precedence order is respected
- Invalid config shows helpful error
- Tests pass for all precedence scenarios

title: Integration tests: event flows and state transitions
description:
**Phase 8: Testing & Release**

Integration tests for event->intent->state->cmd flows across features.

**Test Scenarios:**

**Outline Flow:**
- Open outline (o)
- Type filter query
- Move selection (j/k)
- Preview updates viewport
- Confirm (Enter) commits jump
- Cancel (Esc) restores origin

**Search Flow:**
- Open search (/)
- Type query
- Submit (Enter)
- Verify matches found
- Navigate matches (Ctrl+n/p)
- Close search, highlights remain

**Link Navigation Flow:**
- Tab to focus link
- Follow link (Enter)
- New doc loads
- Back (b) returns to origin
- Forward (Ctrl+i) goes to second doc

**Session Flow:**
- Open doc, move cursor
- Quit and save session
- Restart, verify position restored

**Stale Result Rejection:**
- Start search
- Start new search immediately
- Verify old results discarded
- Verify only new results shown

**Testing Approach:**
- Simulate user input events
- Assert state changes
- Verify commands emitted
- Verify final state

**Acceptance:**
- All integration tests pass
- State transitions correct
- Cancellation works
- Tests cover major workflows

title: Unit tests: parser, navigation, search
description:

**Parser Tests:**

title: Implement search highlighting and match navigation
description:
**Phase 4: Search**

Add visual highlighting for search matches and implement next/prev match navigation.

**Highlighting:**
- Current match: distinct background color (yellow/orange)
- Other matches: subtle highlight (grey/dim yellow)
- Not color-only: use underline or brackets too

**Match Navigation:**
- Ctrl+n: jump to next match (wrap at end)
- Ctrl+p: jump to previous match (wrap at start)
- Update current_match_idx
- Scroll viewport to show current match
- Update status: "Match 3 of 15"

**Rendering Integration:**
Overlay highlights on DisplayLine spans:
```rust
fn apply_search_highlights(
    line: &DisplayLine,
    matches: &[SearchMatch],
    current: Option<usize>,
) -> Vec<StyledSpan>
```

**Testing:**
- Search and verify highlighting
- Navigate matches with Ctrl+n/p
- Verify wrapping at boundaries
- Test current vs other match colors

**Acceptance:**
- Matches are highlighted correctly
- Current match visually distinct
- Navigation works and wraps
- Viewport follows current match

title: Implement link following for anchors and local files
Implement bookmarks (mark and palette)
description:
**Phase 6: Help & Bookmarks**

Implement bookmark system: 'm' to mark current location, '\' to open bookmark palette for jumping.

**Bookmark Model:**
```rust
struct Bookmark {
    key: char,  // Single-char label (a-z, 0-9)
    doc_id: DocumentId,
    path: Option<PathBuf>,
    line: usize,
    timestamp: SystemTime,
}

struct BookmarkState {
    bookmarks: HashMap<char, Bookmark>,
}
```

**Mark (m):**
- Press 'm': show prompt "Mark: _"
- User types single char (a-z or 0-9)
- Store current doc + cursor position
- Status: "Bookmark 'a' set"
- Overwrite if key exists (with confirmation?)

**Palette ('\''):**
- Show modal with bookmark list
- Display: Key | File | Line | Timestamp
- j/k to navigate, Enter to jump, Esc to close
- Filter by typing

**Jump:**
- Look up bookmark by key
- If doc already loaded: jump to line
- If doc not loaded: load doc then jump
- Handle missing files gracefully

**Testing:**
- Create bookmarks
- Open palette and jump
- Test bookmark persistence
- Test missing file handling

**Acceptance:**
- Can create and jump to bookmarks
- Palette lists bookmarks correctly
- Jump works for current and other docs

title: Accessibility audit: keyboard-only and focus indicators
description:
**Phase 7: Accessibility & Polish**

Comprehensive accessibility audit ensuring all features work keyboard-only with clear focus indicators.

**Keyboard-Only Requirements:**
- Every feature accessible without mouse
- No hover-only interactions
- Clear focus states for all interactive elements
- Tab order logical and predictable

**Focus Indicators:**
- Links: underline + symbol (not color alone)
- Modal selections: > arrow + background
- Input fields: blinking cursor + border
- Buttons: brackets or highlight
- All indicators visible in high-contrast

**Testing Matrix:**
- Complete core workflows keyboard-only
- Outline: open, filter, select, confirm
- Search: open, type, navigate results
- Links: tab, follow, back
- Help: open, filter, close
- Bookmarks: mark, palette, jump

**High-Contrast Verification:**
- Test with high-contrast theme
- Ensure text meets WCAG contrast ratios
- Status messages readable
- Focus indicators distinct

**Screen Size Testing:**
- 80x24 (minimum)
- 100x30 (typical)
- 200x60 (large)
- Verify all modals fit
- Verify text truncation graceful

**Acceptance:**
- All features work keyboard-only
- Focus always visible and clear
- High-contrast mode works
- Works at 80x24 minimum

title: Implement link focus cycling (Tab/Shift+Tab)
description:
**Phase 5: Link Navigation**

Implement Tab/Shift+Tab cycling through visible links with focus highlighting.

**Link Focus State:**
```rust
pub struct LinkState {
    pub focused_link_id: Option<LinkId>,
    pub visible_links: Vec<LinkId>,  // Links in current viewport
    pub focus_index: usize,
}
```

**Focus Cycling:**
- Tab: advance focus_index, wrap at end
- Shift+Tab: retreat focus_index, wrap at start
- Update focused_link_id from visible_links
- Scroll viewport if focused link not fully visible

**Visible Link Calculation:**
```rust
fn calculate_visible_links(doc: &Document, viewport: &ViewportState) -> Vec<LinkId> {
    let visible_range = viewport.visible_lines();
    doc.links.iter()
        .filter(|link| visible_range.contains(&link.line_number))
        .map(|link| link.id)
        .collect()
}
```

**Focus Highlighting:**
- Focused link: distinct underline + background
- Not color-only: use >> markers or bold
- Clear visual distinction from normal links

**Edge Cases:**
- No links in document
- No links in viewport
- Viewport scrolls, visible links change
- Link at viewport boundary

**Testing:**
- Tab through links
- Verify focus cycles
- Verify highlighting
- Test viewport with no links
- Test scrolling updates visible links

**Acceptance:**
- Tab/Shift+Tab cycle links correctly
- Focus wraps at boundaries
- Highlighting is clear and accessible
- Visible links update on scroll

title: Performance tuning and optimization
description:
**Phase 7: Accessibility & Polish**

Optimize hot paths to meet performance budgets from plan section 8.1.

**Performance Budgets:**
- Open + index 1MB file: p50 <120ms, p95 <180ms
- Open + index 10MB file: p95 <1.2s
- Heading jump: p95 <5ms
- Outline open (5k headings): p95 <30ms
- Search 10MB file: p95 <400ms, UI responsive
- Memory: <180MB RSS for 10MB file

**Profiling:**
- Use cargo-flamegraph or perf
- Identify hot paths in:
  - Markdown parsing
  - Display projection
  - Search execution
  - Rendering pipeline

**Optimization Techniques:**
- Binary search for heading lookup (already planned)
- Cache display projection by viewport width
- Reuse buffers in search
- Limit link focus calc to viewport
- Incremental viewport rendering

**Memory Optimization:**
- Arc for shared data (already planned)
- Limit history stack depth
- Clear stale display cache entries

**Benchmarking:**
- cargo bench for critical paths
- Load fixtures: 1MB, 10MB, 50MB
- Measure parse, index, search, render times
- Compare against budgets

**Acceptance:**
- All budgets met or documented exceptions
- Benchmarks run in CI
- No performance regressions
- Smooth UX even with large docs

title: CI/CD pipeline setup
description:
**Phase 8: Testing & Release**

Set up CI/CD pipeline for automated testing and quality gates.

**CI Workflow:**
- Trigger on: push, pull request
- Matrix: Linux (Ubuntu), macOS
- Rust versions: stable, beta

**CI Jobs:**

**1. Format Check:**
```
cargo fmt --check
```

**2. Clippy Lints:**
```
cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings
```

**3. Build:**
```
cargo build --release
```

**4. Test:**
```
cargo test
cargo test --release -- --ignored  # Large fixtures
```

**5. Benchmarks (smoke):**
```
cargo bench -- --quick
```

**Platform-Specific:**
- Key event tests on both Linux and macOS
- Terminal compatibility checks

**Artifacts:**
- Upload release binaries
- Store benchmark results

**Status Checks:**
- All jobs must pass for merge
- Format, clippy, and tests are required

**Configuration:**
- GitHub Actions workflow file
- Or: use cargo-make for local + CI

**Acceptance:**
- CI pipeline configured
- All checks pass
- Runs on push/PR
- Cross-platform tested

title: Build heading extraction and indexing
description:
**Phase 1: Markdown Parsing**

Extract heading structure from parsed markdown events and build a queryable heading index.

**Background:**
Headings are the primary navigation structure in mdmd. Users jump between headings with n/p, use outline view to scan structure, and status bar shows current heading context. The heading index must be accurate and efficient.

**Heading Model:**

```rust
pub struct Heading {
    pub id: HeadingId,           // Unique identifier
    pub level: u8,                // 1-6 for H1-H6
    pub title: String,            // Display text
    pub line_number: usize,       // Line in source
    pub start_byte: usize,        // Byte offset start
    pub end_byte: usize,          // Byte offset end
    pub slug: String,             // URL-safe anchor
    pub parent: Option<HeadingId>, // Parent heading (for hierarchy)
}

pub struct HeadingIndex {
    headings: Vec<Heading>,       // Sorted by line_number
    by_slug: HashMap<String, Vec<HeadingId>>, // For anchor lookup
}
```

**Extraction Algorithm:**
1. Walk parsed events looking for Event::Start(Tag::Heading{level})
2. Collect text until Event::End(Tag::Heading)
3. Record line number and byte span
4. Generate slug from heading text
5. Determine parent based on level (track stack of open headings)

**Slug Generation:**
- Convert to lowercase
- Replace spaces with hyphens
- Remove special characters (keep alphanumeric and hyphens)
- Handle unicode correctly
- Must match GitHub's slug algorithm for compatibility

**Parent Hierarchy:**
- Maintain stack of open headings by level
- When seeing H3, parent is most recent H2 or H1
- When seeing H2, parent is most recent H1
- Enables outline tree rendering

**Edge Cases:**
- Headings inside code blocks (must skip)
- Empty heading text
- Duplicate slugs (handled in separate task)
- Headings with inline code or formatting
- Very deep nesting (level 6+)

**Testing:**
- Extract headings from simple markdown
- Verify slug generation matches GitHub
- Test parent hierarchy is correct
- Test headings inside code blocks are ignored
- Test empty and malformed headings
- Unicode heading text

**Performance:**
- O(n) extraction with document size
- O(log n) lookup by line number (binary search)
- O(1) lookup by slug (with duplicate handling)

**Acceptance:**
- Correctly extracts all headings from fixtures
- Slugs match GitHub format
- Parent hierarchy is correct
- Tests pass for all edge cases
- Heading navigation will work (n/p)

title: Implement background search worker with cancellation
description:
**Phase 4: Search**

Add background worker for searching large documents without blocking UI, with generation-based cancellation.

**Background:**
Documents >2500 lines or >256KB trigger background search. Worker runs on separate thread, UI remains responsive, shows "Searching..." status.

**Worker Design:**
```rust
pub enum Cmd {
    Search { request_id: RequestId, generation: Generation, query: SearchQuery },
    CancelSearch { generation: Generation },
}

pub enum Msg {
    SearchProgress { generation: Generation, partial: Vec<SearchMatch> },
    SearchComplete { request_id: RequestId, generation: Generation, matches: Vec<SearchMatch> },
}
```

**Cancellation:**
- Each search has unique generation number
- New search increments generation
- Worker checks generation periodically
- Stale results (old generation) are discarded in reducer

**UI Feedback:**
- Show "Searching..." spinner in status
- Update periodically with partial results (optional)
- Show "Search cancelled" if superseded
- Show "N matches found" on completion

**Testing:**
- Search large document (10MB fixture)
- Verify UI stays responsive
- Start new search while old is running, verify cancellation
- Verify stale results are discarded
- Performance: search completes in <400ms for 10MB

**Acceptance:**
- Background search works for large docs
- UI remains responsive during search
- Cancellation works correctly
- No stale results corrupt state

title: Integrate pulldown-cmark parser with event collection
description:
**Phase 1: Markdown Parsing**

Integrate pulldown-cmark to parse markdown into events with source position tracking.

**Background:**
pulldown-cmark is the de-facto Rust markdown parser. It provides a streaming API that emits events (Start/End/Text/Code) with byte offsets. We need to collect these events to build our structural index.

**Requirements:**
- Add pulldown-cmark dependency to Cargo.toml
- Create parser module (src/parser/mod.rs)
- Implement event collection with source spans
- Track line boundaries for line-to-offset mapping
- Preserve all markdown constructs (headings, links, code blocks, etc.)

**Implementation Strategy:**

```rust
pub struct ParsedEvent {
    pub event: Event<'static>,
    pub start_byte: usize,
    pub end_byte: usize,
    pub line_number: usize,
}

pub fn parse_markdown(source: &str) -> Vec<ParsedEvent> {
    let parser = Parser::new_ext(source, Options::all());
    let parser = parser.into_offset_iter();
    // Collect events with positions
}
```

**Key Decisions:**
- Enable all extensions (tables, strikethrough, footnotes)
- Use offset_iter for byte position tracking
- Store owned events (Event<'static>) for lifetime simplicity
- Build line map alongside event collection

**Edge Cases to Handle:**
- Empty documents
- Documents without headings
- Malformed markdown (parser is lenient)
- Unicode characters and their byte/char offset differences
- Code fences (must not treat their content as markdown)

**Testing:**
- Parse simple markdown fixtures
- Verify byte offsets are correct
- Test unicode handling
- Test code fence content is preserved
- Test empty document handling

**Performance Considerations:**
- Parsing is one-time cost on document load
- Events are collected into Vec (acceptable for documents up to 10MB)
- Line map is O(n) with document size

**Acceptance:**
- Can parse all fixture documents
- Events have accurate byte positions
- Line numbers are correct
- Tests pass for various markdown constructs

title: Create test fixture documents
description:
**Phase 8: Testing & Release**

Generate comprehensive markdown test fixtures covering edge cases.

**Fixtures Needed:**
- simple.md: basic markdown, few headings
- nested_headings.md: deep hierarchy (H1-H6)
- links_internal.md: same-doc anchors, relative links
- links_external.md: http/https URLs
- duplicate_anchors.md: multiple headings with same text
- unicode.md: emoji, CJK characters, RTL text
- code_fences.md: code blocks, inline code
- huge_wrapped_lines.md: lines >1000 chars
- large.md: ~1MB, 10k lines
- massive.md: ~10MB, 100k lines

**Generation:**
- Script to generate large.md and massive.md
- Hand-craft edge case fixtures
- Include in fixtures/ directory
- Document each fixture's purpose

**Usage:**
- Unit tests reference fixtures
- Integration tests use fixtures
- Manual QA uses fixtures
- Benchmark suite uses fixtures

**Acceptance:**
- All fixtures created
- Cover edge cases from plan
- Fixtures documented
- Used in test suite

title: Benchmark suite and performance regression tracking
description:
**Phase 8: Testing & Release**

Build comprehensive benchmark suite to track performance and prevent regressions.

**Benchmarks:**

**1. Open and Index:**
- small.md (10KB)
- large.md (1MB)
- massive.md (10MB)
- Measure: parse + index construction time
- Budgets: see plan section 8.1

**2. Heading Navigation:**
- Binary search lookup on 5k headings
- Budget: p95 <5ms

**3. Outline:**
- Open outline with 5k headings
- Filter outline
- Budget: p95 <30ms

**4. Search:**
- Literal search on 10MB doc
- Regex search on 10MB doc
- Budget: p95 <400ms

**5. Rendering:**
- Full viewport render (50 lines)
- Display cache hit/miss times

**Implementation:**
- Use criterion.rs for statistical benchmarks
- Store baseline results
- CI compares against baseline
- Fail if regression >10%

**Fixture Generation:**
- Generate deterministic large fixtures
- Include in fixtures/ for reproducibility

**CI Integration:**
- Run benchmarks on every PR
- Comment with comparison results
- Alert on significant regressions

**Acceptance:**
- Benchmark suite covers hot paths
- Baselines established
- CI tracks regressions
- All budgets met or documented

title: Implement search state and prompt UI
description:
**Phase 4: Search**

Build search state model and prompt UI for text search with case/regex mode toggles.

**State Model:**
```rust
pub struct SearchState {
    pub is_active: bool,
    pub query: String,
    pub matches: Vec<SearchMatch>,
    pub current_match_idx: Option<usize>,
    pub case_mode: CaseMode,
    pub use_regex: bool,
    pub generation: u64,  // For cancellation
    pub is_searching: bool,  // Async in progress
}

pub struct SearchMatch {
    pub line: usize,
    pub col_start: usize,
    pub col_end: usize,
    pub text: String,
}

pub enum CaseMode {
    Insensitive,
    Sensitive,
    Smart,  // Case-sensitive if query has uppercase
}
```

**Prompt UI:**
Bottom-overlay search bar:
```
Search: rust_  [Case: insensitive] [Regex: off]
```

**Interactions:**
- /: open search prompt
- Type: update query
- Backspace: delete char
- Enter: execute search
- Esc: cancel
- Alt+c: toggle case mode
- Alt+r: toggle regex mode
- Ctrl+n: next match
- Ctrl+p: previous match

**Lifecycle:**
1. Press /: open prompt, clear query
2. Type query: update live (optional debounce)
3. Press Enter: execute search, jump to first match
4. Ctrl+n/p: navigate matches
5. Esc: close prompt, keep matches highlighted

**Testing:**
- Open/close prompt
- Type query and verify display
- Toggle case/regex modes
- Execute search
- Navigate matches

**Acceptance:**
- Search prompt renders
- Query input works
- Mode toggles work
- State tracks matches correctly

title: Implement outline live preview and confirm/cancel
Implement basic frankentui app loop scaffold
description:
**Phase 0: Bootstrap**

Create the main application loop using frankentui's Elm architecture pattern with minimal Model, update, and view functions.

**Background:**
frankentui provides an Elm-style reactive architecture:
- Model: holds all application state
- init: creates initial model
- update: pure function (Model, Msg) -> (Model, Cmd)  
- view: pure function Model -> UI tree
- Event loop handles terminal events, dispatches to update, renders view

**Initial Scaffold:**

```rust
pub struct Model {
    config: Config,
    should_quit: bool,
    screen_mode: ScreenMode,
    // More fields added in later tasks
}

pub fn init(config: Config) -> (Model, Cmd) {
    // Initialize model from config
}

pub fn update(model: &mut Model, msg: Msg) -> Cmd {
    // Handle messages and return commands
}

pub fn view(model: &Model) -> Element {
    // Render UI (minimal for now)
}
```

**Integration Points:**
- Wire up to frankentui's main loop
- Handle terminal initialization (alt-screen vs inline)
- Set up panic handler for terminal cleanup
- Handle Ctrl+C gracefully
- Implement basic quit on 'q' key

**Error Handling:**
- Terminal initialization failures
- Terminal restore on panic
- Clear error messages to stderr

**Design Considerations:**
- Model will grow significantly in later phases
- update function will dispatch to sub-modules
- view will compose widget hierarchy
- Keep this scaffold minimal but extensible

**Testing:**
- Can initialize and quit cleanly
- Alt-screen mode works
- Inline mode works (via flag)
- Panic handler restores terminal
- Quit command works

**Acceptance:**
- App starts and shows empty screen
- 'q' quits cleanly
- Terminal is properly restored
- No panics on normal operation
- cargo run works

title: Implement search engine with literal and regex modes
description:
**Phase 4: Search**

Build unified search engine supporting literal substring and regex search with proper unicode handling.

**Search Algorithm:**

Literal search:
```rust
fn search_literal(source: &str, query: &str, case_mode: CaseMode) -> Vec<SearchMatch> {
    let source_search = match case_mode {
        CaseMode::Insensitive => source.to_lowercase(),
        _ => source.to_string(),
    };
    let query_search = match case_mode {
        CaseMode::Insensitive => query.to_lowercase(),
        _ => query.to_string(),
    };
    
    // Find all occurrences
    // Track line/col positions
    // Return matches
}
```

Regex search:
- Use regex crate with proper error handling
- Support common patterns
- Catch invalid regex and show error
- Honor case_mode flag

**Unicode Handling:**
- Use char boundaries (not byte offsets)
- Match can span unicode graphemes
- Column positions in characters, not bytes

**Performance:**
- Small docs (<2500 lines): inline execution
- Large docs: background worker (next task)
- Generation tracking prevents stale results

**Testing:**
- Search for simple strings
- Test case-sensitive/insensitive
- Test regex patterns
- Test unicode queries
- Test invalid regex handling

**Acceptance:**
- Literal search finds all matches
- Regex search works correctly
- Unicode handling correct
- Invalid regex shows clear error
